ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general shrimp cake entry; the deep-fried Thai-style preparation is not explicitly distinguished. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Thai prawn cake code. A0CKP (processed crustacean product) is the closest defensible anchor. Deep-fried preparation and flour/starch coating are noted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Thai-style prawn cake as a distinct food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 4 pieces of prawn cake, weighing approximately 200 g, typically served as an appetizer or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp cake, fried (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); supplemented with HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition of Food for sodium and seasoning adjustments
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai-style homemade prawn cake exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried shrimp cake / prawn patty entries and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore seafood fried snack data. Sodium is elevated to 600 mg/100 g to reflect fish sauce and fish paste seasoning typical of Thai-style preparation. Input record values (protein 15 g, fat 20 g, carbohydrate 12.5 g, energy 290 kcal per 100 g) are consistent with this adapted profile and are retained. Calcium 40 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 125 mg, saturated fat 5 g, trans fat 0.25 g, dietary fibre 0.5 g, sugar 2.5 g, and vitamin C 0.0 mg are sourced from the adapted USDA/HPB composite.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on fried fish cake / mixed seafood patty with starch binder
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style homemade prawn cake. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food: fried fish cake with starch binder, reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al.). GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification system, which defines low GI as 55 or below, medium GI as 56–69, and high GI as 70 or above. Accordingly gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (25 g from 200 g portion) / 100 = 13.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 600 mg per 100 g; a 200 g serving delivers 1200 mg sodium — approximately 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit — driven by fish sauce and fish paste seasoning.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to 1–2 pieces per meal and pair with potassium-rich sides such as steamed vegetables to partially offset the high sodium load; home preparation should use reduced fish sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (55) with 12.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g from refined starch/flour coating; high fat content (20 g/100 g) from deep-frying may impair postprandial insulin sensitivity despite the low GI value.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to 1–2 pieces per meal, choose baked or air-fried alternatives to reduce fat load, and monitor postprandial glucose response given the refined carbohydrate coating and high fat content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 20 g/100 g with saturated fat 5 g/100 g and trans fat 0.25 g/100 g from deep-frying; cholesterol 125 mg/100 g; sodium 600 mg/100 g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this deep-fried preparation; if consumed, limit to 1 piece occasionally and prioritise steamed or baked prawn alternatives to reduce saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; saturated fat at 5 g/100 g further promotes pro-inflammatory pathways.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried prawn cakes due to AGE formation and opt for steamed or baked prawn alternatives to minimise inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai-Style Homemade Prawn Cake (自制虾饼; Tod Man Goong) is a deep-fried seafood snack popular in Singapore's Thai-influenced food scene. Each cake is made from minced or blended prawn combined with fish paste, tapioca or wheat starch, red curry paste, fish sauce, kaffir lime leaves, and egg, shaped into small round patties and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining moist and springy inside. A standard serving is 4 pieces weighing approximately 200 g. Per 100 g, the dish provides 290 kcal, 15 g protein, 20 g fat (5 g saturated, 0.25 g trans), 12.5 g carbohydrate, 2.5 g sugar, 0.5 g dietary fibre, and 600 mg sodium. The high sodium content (600 mg/100 g) is driven by fish sauce and fish paste, making this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension. The deep-frying process elevates total and saturated fat, raising cardiovascular risk. The low glycemic index (estimated ~55, at the upper boundary of the low GI range) combined with refined starch coating and high fat content requires portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean, fish, or egg allergies. It is commonly served as an appetizer or side dish in Thai restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai-style homemade prawn cake exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved-source analogues (USDA SR Legacy fried shrimp/prawn cake entries and HPB Singapore seafood fried snack data). Input record values are consistent with this adapted profile and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Thai-style deep-fried prawn cake is not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this preparation is not reported in the adapted USDA/HPB sources consulted. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific Thai-style deep-fried prawn cake preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2024. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central for fried seafood patty preparations; deep-frying destroys residual ascorbic acid. trans_fat_g is 0.25: small but non-zero trans fat from deep-frying in vegetable oil; retained from input record and consistent with USDA data for deep-fried seafood products. Halal status is marked false as fish sauce and preparation method cannot be confirmed halal without restaurant certification. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on fried fish cake with starch binder from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this dish. Per the University of Sydney GI classification, GI ≤55 = low, 56–69 = medium, ≥70 = high; GI 55 therefore falls in the low category and gi_category has been corrected to 'low' from the previous iteration's erroneous 'medium'. Glycemic load per serving = 55 × 25 g carbohydrate (200 g portion) / 100 = 13.75.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Thai-Style Homemade Prawn Cake' is precise and non-promotional. Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Thai local names are all present. Singaporean dish multilingual requirement satisfied. Thai script (ทอดมันกุ้ง) present for Thai cuisine origin.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (290), protein_g (15), fat_g (20), carbohydrate_g (12.5) all present and numeric. Nutrient source documented as USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy adapted with HPB Singapore cross-reference. Source match type is 'adapted' with full explanation.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.5), sodium_mg (600), saturated_fat_g (5.0), sugar_g (2.5) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible for a deep-fried Thai prawn cake and consistent with adapted USDA/HPB sources.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (55), gi_category ('low' — corrected from previous iteration's erroneous 'medium'; GI 55 is at the upper boundary of the low range per University of Sydney classification: low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), glycemic_load_per_serving (13.75), and gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via analogous estimate (fried fish cake with starch binder, Atkinson et al. 2008). Estimation basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided (FOODON_03302051, shrimp cake food product) with 'closest_parent' match type and gap note. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (processed crustacean product) provided with 'closest_parent' match type and gap note. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note. No codes invented; all are defensible anchors.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,100 characters). It covers what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance (hypertension, diabetes, CVD), and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly. Additional condition rheumatoid_arthritis included from input.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains 19 entries covering English variants, Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Thai script, and romanized forms. aliases_by_language is fully populated for all relevant languages. Self-check confirmed: every string in aliases_by_language appears verbatim in the flat aliases array.

Iteration 2: Updated ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' as per G5 hard gate requirements.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI value of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range per University of Sydney classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Updated gi_source notes, health_context type2_diabetes key_factor, llm_training natural_language_description, and notes field to reflect the corrected gi_category.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8. Primary fix in iteration 3: gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index of 55, which sits at the upper boundary of the low GI range per University of Sydney classification. Food identity is canonical with full Singaporean and Thai multilingual coverage. Core macros and secondary nutrients are complete and sourced from USDA FoodData Central / HPB Singapore adapted composite. GI is an approved analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Ontology anchors are defensible with honest gap notes. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Aliases are comprehensive and internally consistent. All -1 sentinel values are documented in notes.
